data.title: IMO's Net-Zero Strategy For Global Shipping Starts With Pricing Carbon But It's Not Perfect
data.description:
By Rico Luman

The IMO has adopted a framework that puts a price on carbon exceeding target levels

On Friday, 11 April, the Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC) of the International Maritime Organization (IMO) agreed upon a set of mid-term measures to get the sector on track to net-zero by 2050. This follows the global shipping regulator's earlier implementation of short-term measures focused on fuel efficiency. The package is due to be adopted by October 2025, with details and implementation guidelines to be specified and approved in spring 2026, before being included in the MARPOL treaty and coming into force in 2027.

The most important elements of the net-zero framework are:

  • Carbon price on target-exceeding emissions: The framework sets a price of $380 per tonne of CO2 equivalent for emissions that fail to meet the base target of an 8% greenhouse gas reduction by 2030, increasing to a 30% reduction by 2035 compared to 2008 levels (starting in 2027). For emissions falling short of a 21% reduction by 2030, running up to 2040 but below the base target, a charge of $100 is applied (see chart). Shipping companies could end up paying up to $1200 per tonne of bunker fuel emitted above the base target, which would quadruple current prices and force action. This means that the largest fraction of shipping emissions remains out of scope. Transport and Environment expects the framework to generate some $10 billion in revenues until 2035. The charges will be redistributed to support greening. What this will look like exactly still needs to be worked out.
  • Rewarding fast movers: Companies outperforming the framework in terms of GHG reduction will receive surplus units, which they could save or sell.
  • Fuel agnostic: The framework introduces a global fuel standard and takes a well-to-wake approach. It doesn’t exclude fuels or particular feedstocks, but focuses on greenhouse gas intensity in grams CO2-equivalent per Mega Joule (gCO2eq/MJ).

Exceeding targeted GHG reduction factors will be priced according to two tiers

Targeted reduction of GHG emissions per year on a company level and associated levy per tonne in case of exceeding (reference: 2008)

But the policy fails to introduce a pricing mechanism covering all shipping emissions

Although the willingness to make progress on pricing measures is encouraging, the proposed framework doesn’t introduce full carbon pricing as implemented under the European Union’s ETS for shipping. This would only make bunker fuel less attractive, it generates much more budget to support greening as well. Several market players, including leading global container liners and shippers such as Cargill and Trafigura, have previously advocated for putting a price on all emissions as this could be a forceful instrument to support decarbonisation. Maersk called for a levy starting from $150-200 and preferably even significantly higher to support the transition. However, this also raises the costs of shipping, and adopting this was probably not achievable at the global level at this point. From an economic point of view, though, this would ultimately be necessary to narrow the gap between bunker fuels and costlier renewable fuels like green methanol and ammonia, and support investments in infrastructure and availability.

Fuel neutrality risks putting biofuels at the forefront

The net-zero framework takes a well-to-wake approach and looks at the greenhouse gas intensity of the fuels that companies use. It’s still unclear which fuel options will be eligible for subsidies and to what amount, but the agreement doesn’t exclude alternative fuels (and includes LNG as well). As such, companies are also allowed to use biofuels, which are often the cheapest and easiest lower-carbon option as they can be used in the existing fleet and don’t require investments in new technology. This will boost demand, while demand from the aviation sector also starts to mount. It also raises questions about controversial (first-generation) feedstocks without specific requirements. Moreover, it could distract from investments in alternatives.

Targets fall short of earlier ambitions

In terms of target setting, the IMO previously adopted a goal of reducing GHG emissions by 20%, striving for 30% by 2030, and 70%, striving for 80% by 2040, both compared to 2008. The adopted framework seems to focus on a reduction of at least 8%-21% by 2030, which looks less ambitious. At the same time, total absolute emissions in shipping have risen in recent years, underscoring the need for more decisive action.

All in all, I believe this framework is a step in the right direction, but it should also be seen as a framework to build upon further down the line.

At the general sector level, global shipping GHG emissions have increased in the last couple of years

Index 2008 = 100

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: HomeStreet: Loan Portfolio Repositioning To Support Profitability, Sustainability, And Upside
data.description:
Banks are often the first to feel the impact of macroeconomic volatility, making them vulnerable to uncertainties and recessions. Despite this, many banks are starting to learn how to position themselves against these risks to improve financial stability and operational sustainability. An excellent example is HomeStreet, Inc. (NASDAQ:HMST). With its improved loan portfolio diversification and deposit base management, it shows increased resilience amid uncertainties. At its current price with a reasonable upside, this stock can be an ideal addition to your portfolio.

HMST Q4 2024: Lower But Much Better

HomeStreet, Inc. closed the last quarter of 2024 with notable adjustments in its portfolio coupled with drastic monetary policy changes by the Fed.

Its interest income amounted to $99.1M, down by -2.2% YoY from $101.3M, mainly due to policy rate cuts. In only four months, the Fed cut the interest rate by 100 bps from 5.5% to 4.5%. Interest income on investments fell from $11.7M to $9.1M as most of these are debt securities. Note that its yield decreased from 3.94% to 3.63%.

Meanwhile, interest income on loans decreased by only -1.1%. But it was driven by its sale of multifamily loans. And I think this is a prudent move, considering that this helped improve loan yields by 2.0 bps.

Moreover, interest expense decreased due to lower borrowing yields amid rate cuts. Interest in deposits did not even flinch, which was due to the composition of HMST. I will also discuss this later.

In the end, its net interest income dropped by -14.2% YoY, which shows that HMST’s earning assets are more sensitive to rate cuts than deposits and borrowings. But QoQ, it rose by 3.5%, supported by HMST’s prudent loan portfolio adjustments to stabilize profitability in a volatile environment.

HMST’s Drivers

Despite its weaker YoY performance, I believe HMST can deliver a better performance this 2025. These are some drivers.

Improved Loan Portfolio Diversification

I like the fact that HMST has a higher exposure to commercial loans (76%) than consumer loans (24%). Commercial loans are less sensitive to policy rate changes, offering more income stability. They are more secure, so delinquency risks are more manageable than in consumer loans. As we can see, loan yields improved by 2.00 bps despite the massive reduction in loans and the US interest rate.

I like it better today, as HMST appears to be learning to minimize risks by repositioning its loan portfolio. One crucial move took place in 2024 after its sale of $990M in multifamily in CRE loans. In my view, this is a strategic decision to improve company profitability, stability, and sustainability. While retail and industrial remained strong and viable, CRE properties like multifamily and offices are already at risk of devaluation.

In a recent study, multi-family properties had a notable decrease in asset value from 2021 to 2024. If we trace it back to 2019, its asset value is still lower by 6.1% or $300B. In addition, multifamily returns have already dropped by 6.5%.

While the company did not disclose which portion of these loans have fixed and variable rates or which are set for repricing, I believe its portfolio is positioned for risks. After all, interest and mortgage rates are still elevated. So disposing of risky loans will allow it to improve collection efficiency and interest income as yields become more stable today.

Improved Liquidity

Liquidity and sustainability also improved after HMST sold its multifamily loans. One indication is the decrease in its loan-to-deposit ratio from 109% to 96%. At this point, HMST has some reserves to make for delinquencies and defaults. In addition, the proceeds also helped reduce borrowings by 43% YoY. Today, it has $1.0B in borrowings versus $1.74B in 2023. In addition, 55% of its borrowings have longer maturities. As a result, HMST may incur lower interest expense on borrowings this 2025.

Moreover, I like that over 50% of its deposits are time deposits. These typically have fixed rates, so rate hikes and cuts do not necessarily affect them. We must note the potential risks associated with Trump’s tariffs, which can be inflationary and affect the Fed’s decision in the long run. So even if the policy easing cycle reverses if tariffs become unmanageable, time deposits may remain stable. In addition, it can limit deposit runs since they can’t be withdrawn anytime, unlike demand deposits.

Capital Adequacy

Its capital adequacy may have decreased recently, but we are certain that HMST can cover risks. Its CET 1 Ratio of 12.3% shows that high-quality capital can cover that portion of risk-weighted assets. Its Total Capital Ratio of 13.0% is much higher than what Basel III requires, which is 8.0%. So, HMST can withstand recessions or financial crises even without increasing its borrowings. Lastly, its Tier 1 leverage of 7.3% proves that HMST is a well-capitalized bank in the US. This shows that HMST’s current capital strength against the total assets is enough. It also ensures that HMST is not overleveraging today. With an adequate capital buffer, HSMT can prevent deposit runs and insolvency.

Price Ratio

As a bank, HMST may be valued using the P/B Ratio. In the past four years, we have seen the price crashing. The stock is now 76% lower than its average price in 2021. Relative to the BVPS, I think the downtrend was logical. However, the actual rate of decrease seems too much. Currently, it is trading at about 0.50x its BVPS, which is much lower than the ideal maximum of 1.00x. It is also lower than the five-year average of 0.87x. To be fair, the stock has been reasonably cheap within these years. If we use the average P/B Ratio to derive its fair value, the TP will be $18.41, a 74% upside potential.

I think a rebound is possible considering its high responsiveness to BVPS. After tracing back on how the price reacted to BVPS, they came up with a 70% correlation. Note that I used semi-annual values to have at least 20 observations. This shows that as BVPS increases or decreases, there is a 70% chance of the price going in the same direction. In addition, its p-value of only 0.024 means that only 2.4% of their relationship happened by chance, which erases the randomness of the data.

Technicals

HMST is showing more improvements after its bullish fair value gap two weeks ago. It is already trading above the 50-day SMA. In addition, the 50-day SMA appears to be increasing again after the Death Cross last December. Both instances show improved market sentiments recently. But traders must still be cautious as the price and the 50-day SMA stay below the 200-day SMA.

Meanwhile, technical indicators like MACD and RSI have shown a momentum rebound recently. But they also advise caution as the stock hits the oversold region. Meanwhile, the MACD line just crossed below the signal line and the histogram hits the negative region again. They may signal new selling pressures after a new high of $12.55 enticed profit takers.

Despite this, investors may position comfortably at the current price. As you can see, the RSI is now back to 52, which shows that the room for buying expands again. We also saw a bullish rejection last April 11, showing that investors rejected any price lower than $10.43 and that buyers defended the support. After all, selling pressures have weakened in the past few days, showing that buyers are stepping in again. At this point, we can see the market’s indecision with the spinning top candle, as if testing whether the recent rebound will be sustained. This can be proven by the longer upper wick, implying that buyers are trying to push the price up again.

Conclusion

HomeStreet, Inc. is a well-positioned bank today in the current market environment. Its loan portfolio changes, solid customer base, and well-capitalized operations show sustainability. In addition, the price is much cheaper today with a decent upside potential. Technicals advise us to trade with caution, but buying opportunities are present. I am giving this stock a buy rating.

data.title: Consumers Back In Splurge Mode, Retail Sales Surge - Even At Restaurants And Bars Not Part Of Frontrunning Tariffs
data.description:
The recession is going to have to get back in line and wait.

Retail sales in March jumped majestically, and January and February were revised higher. And it wasn’t just motor vehicle sales and tariff-frontrunning, or whatever, but also sales in restaurants and bars where sales jumped - a sign that consumers are splurging, not frontrunning. They’re in a foul mood in sentiment surveys because they hate high prices and inflation, but they’re out there having fun and spending money. The recession is going to have to get back in line and wait.

Retail sales jumped by 1.4% seasonally adjusted in March from upwardly revised February sales, and January was upwardly revised as well, to a record $735 billion, up by 4.8% year-over-year (red in the chart below).

Not seasonally adjusted, January and February are the worst months of the year in retail sales. December is by far the best month of the year (blue in the chart below). Massive seasonal adjustments attempt to iron this out by slashing December sales and by raising January and February sales. But if these huge seasonal adjustments go awry even a little, the seasonally adjusted results can trigger recession calls, then March comes along with almost no seasonal adjustments, and everything is fine again. This is a drama that gets rerun in Q1 in many years.

Non-frontrunning splurge: Sales at restaurants and bars. Food services and drinking places include everything from cafeterias to restaurants and bars. It’s the third-largest category of retailers behind auto & parts dealers and nonstore retailers (mostly ecommerce). It accounts for 13% of total retail sales.

Most of this is discretionary spending, money that consumers want to spend but don’t have to spend. And it’s unrelated to tariffs and any efforts to frontrun the tariffs.

Not seasonally adjusted, sales at these establishments spiked by 15.8% in March from February, to a record $102 billion. Seasonally adjusted, they jumped by 1.8% for the month to $98 billion, and were up 4.8% year-over-year.

Our Drunken Sailors, as we’ve come to call them facetiously and lovingly, are back on track. Maybe widespread bad weather kept them at home in January and February, but they splurged when the sun came out in March. And it had nothing to do with frontrunning tariffs:

Tax refunds & frontrunning surge: New and used vehicle dealers and parts stores, the largest retailer category with 19% of total retail sales, had a big month.

Not seasonally adjusted, sales spiked by 24% in March from February, to a record $154 billion. March is often the best month of the year; February and January are by far the worst months of the year. Year-over-year, sales spiked by 9.8% (blue in the chart below).

Seasonally adjusted, sales surged by 5.3% in March from February and by 8.8% year-over-year. Seasonal adjustments also adjust for the difference in “selling days” (red).

We knew this surge would be happening: New vehicle sales, in terms of the number of vehicles sold, had shown decent growth in January and February and had spiked 13% year-over-year in March for the best Q1 since 2019. And sales of used vehicles also spiked 13% year-over-year in March, after solid growth in January and February.

As I pointed out, this was driven by several factors, among them:

  • Big “tax refund season”: Surging tax refunds ($208 billion through March), well ahead of a year ago, like stimulus checks for downpayments;
  • Tariff fears frontrunning;
  • Urge to buy a nicer set of wheels - very ingrained in our Drunken Sailors.

And the sun came out, and they bought.

Ecommerce and other “nonstore retailers” (ecommerce retailers, ecommerce operations of brick-and-mortar retailers, and stalls and markets, #2 category) only saw modest growth.

Not seasonally adjusted, sales rose by 8.5% in March from February, and by 6.1% year-over-year, to $123 billion. Seasonally adjusted, sales inched up 0.1% in March, and rose by 4.8% year-over-year, to $127 billion.

What, no tariff-frontrunning, suddenly? Ecommerce is where tariff-frontrunning should have also taken place, but apparently it didn’t. This slow growth (for ecommerce) and the splurge at restaurants, which has nothing to do with tariffs, sprinkle doubt on just how much of a factor tariff-frontrunning really was. Maybe people were just in a mood to eat and drink out and buy vehicles? That’s what American consumers are known to do.

Food and Beverage Stores, #4 category of retailer, 12% of retail sales:

  • Sales: $85 billion
  • From prior month: +0.2%
  • Year-over-year: +3.6%

General merchandise stores, minus department stores (#5 category, 9% of retail sales), including retailers such as Walmart (WMT), which is also the largest grocer in the US.

  • Sales: $67 billion
  • From prior month: +0.8%
  • Year-over-year: +4.8%

Gas stations (#6 category, 7% of retail sales). Dollar sales at gas stations move in near-lockstep with the price of gasoline. The price of gasoline started zigzagging lower in mid-2022. These price declines pushed down dollar sales at gas stations. Sales at gas stations also include all the other merchandise gas stations sell:

  • Sales: $51 billion
  • From prior month: -2.5%
  • Year-over-year: -4.3%

Sales in billions of dollars at gas stations (red, left axis); and the CPI for gasoline (blue, right axis):

Building materials, garden supply and equipment stores (#7 category, 6% of total retail). The remodeling boom during the pandemic fizzled in late 2022, and sales fell for a while. But in March, sales spiked, even seasonally adjusted. May is usually the best month:

  • Sales: $41 billion
  • From prior month: +3.3%
  • Year-over-year: +2.6%

Health and personal care stores (#8 category, 5% of total retail):

  • Sales: $39 billion
  • From prior month: +0.7%
  • Year-over-year: +7.2%

Clothing and accessory stores (#9 category, 3.7% of retail):

  • Sales: $27 billion
  • From prior month: +0.4%
  • Year-over-year: +5.4%

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AGNCO And AGNCM Preferred Stock Returns
data.description:
Introduction

About two years ago, I published an article highlighting the potential for double digit returns with AGNC preferred stocks. I presented information on all five of the preferred stocks that AGNC has issued - AGNCL, AGNCM, AGNCN, NASDAQ:AGNCO, and AGNCP. Ultimately, I invested heavily in two - AGNCM and AGNCO. I still hold both of these securities. This article provides an update on those four preferred stocks and real world current returns I was able to achieve on the two preferreds that I am holding.

AGNC Investment Options

Today, investors have a couple different options for investing in AGNC. Purchasing AGNC common stock which is currently trading at $8.46 offering a $1.44 annual dividend for a very fat yield of 17%. Rather interestingly, when I published my earlier article on AGNC preferreds, AGNC was offering the same $1.44 annual dividend with a valuation of $9.75 offering a slightly lower fat yield of 14.77%. Two years ago, had I chosen to invest in AGNC common, I would have an unrecognized capital loss of 15.25% today. The two years worth of dividend payments would have covered the capital loss but, my overall total return would have been much lower than the route I decided to follow, investing in the preferred shares.

AGNC has five outstanding preferred series that are cumulative, four of which are fixed rate to quarterly floating rate conversions and one that is a 5 year reset rate conversion. Series G, AGNCL, converts to a reset rate October 15, 2027 tied to the 5 year Treasury rate plus 4.39%. Two years ago, the 2027 conversion date was about 4.5 years out. Given the cloudiness of my crystal ball at that time, I didn't pursue AGNCL any further. Today, AGNCL trades at $24.15 having dropped from well over par value over the last 10 days. The current 5 year Treasury rate stands at 4.15%, but I don't expect it to be that high in late 2027. I expect rates to be 50 to 75 bps lower giving a 5 year floating conversion of 8–7.75%, essentially the same as the current AGNCL fixed rate. Not enough potential return to interest me.

In my previous article, I took a hard closer look at the remaining four fixed-to-floating rate preferred securities shown in the table below.

Readers should note that this table contains data that is two years old. This is the same table that was published two years ago with a couple of now unnecessary columns deleted. When I published my original article, AGNCN has already converted to a floating rate October 15, 2022 and its price had moved up to near its $25 par value. I viewed AGNCN as not having much potential for returns other than its current yield, and so I dropped it from further consideration.

While I carried AGNCP on my spreadsheet, I chose to not accumulate shares at that time on the thinking that the May 15, 2025 call and conversion date was too far out. I planned to revisit AGNCP at some time in the future. That left me with AGNCM and AGNCO in which I did accumulate fairly substantial holdings. The SA editors picked out AGNCO from my original article as the covered stock for analyst rating, as the figure below shows.

A Solid Double Digit Return

As I mentioned earlier, I still hold AGNCM and AGNCO, and I've received solid double digit returns on both securities. Had I reinvested AGNCO dividends consistent with the SA total return calculation, I'd have done a little better on total return. I didn't reinvest dividends, as I prefer to choose the timing for all my investments. However, as you will see below, my total current unrealized capital gain plus accumulated dividends beat the return on the S&P500 total return by a smidge (33.4% vs 32.6%). That's not too shabby for a conservative boring preferred stock that only widows and orphans should have interest in.

With AGNCM, we did even better with total return and besting the S&P500. Again, no reinvested dividends with AGNCM. My total return to date from unrealized capital gains plus accumulated dividends is 40.7%, 8% better than the S&P500. My current returns for both AGNCM and AGNCO are shown in the table below.

The last column in the table above is labeled "Potential Total Gain (%)". This column represents the total return if the securities were called tomorrow at their $25 par value. Granted, it is a fictitious metric but it does point out that both securities have a bit more capital gain to be had. Readers should note that prior to April 1, 2025, both AGNCM and AGNCO traded well above their $25 par values. Once the market chaos subsides, I fully expect both securities will return to valuations above $25.

I published an article last week entitled Chimera Preferreds: Preferred Stocks Are A Buy Again. In this article, I make the case for CIM-C and CIM-D being investible again as both have dropped significantly below their par values and CIM-C will convert to a higher floating rate at the end of September, 2025. I noted that I initiated a position in CIM-C and added to my holdings of CIM-D.

While I'm not currently accumulating AGNCM or AGNCO, I am watching for the opportunity to do so. I doubt the recent market chaos is over and there may be yet another downturn in preferred stocks in our future. I've been treating these downturns as buying opportunities and I've deployed a lot of cash into equities over the last couple of weeks.

What Are The Risks Of Holding Preferred Stocks

The biggest risk today that could negatively impact preferred stocks is the trade war having a negative impact on the US bond market. We've already had a taste of this and we didn't like it. Almost all preferred stocks, and definitely all those discussed in this article, are perpetual; they have no stated or defined maturity. Also, preferred stocks have either a fixed coupon or a floating rate tied to some reference rate like CME 3-month Term SOFR or the 5-year Treasury. This makes preferred stocks behave like very long duration bonds. As market interest rates rise, particularly long duration bond rates, preferred stock valuations will fall so that their yield maintains its relationship with bond market rates. This is the primary reason preferred stock prices have been depressed over the last 10 days. The downside for me has been loss of unrealized gains in my preferred stock portfolio. The upside is the lower valuations have given us an opportunity to buy during a significant dip.

data.title: EWL: A More Political Stable, But Too Expensive Alternative To The U.S.
data.description:
Introduction

When thinking of Switzerland, most investors instantly think about stability. As a country that has stayed neutral on most issues, Switzerland can be seen as a safe haven from political turmoil in times like this. This article will show why Switzerland does this assessment justice. However, it will also show why the iShares MSCI Switzerland ETF (NYSEARCA:EWL) is unlikely to be a good investment at the moment, as it is simply too cheap.

Key ETF Data

EWL has an expense ratio of 0.5%, which is cheap compared to other country ETFs, while currently paying no dividend. When looking at valuation, EWL is very expensive at a P/E ratio of 21.18 and a P/B ratio of 3.31, which is almost as high as that of the S&P 500. In comparison, the iShares Europe ETF (IEV), representing European stocks in general, has a P/E and a P/B ratio of 15.69 and 1.89 respectively, highlighting the huge premium Swiss stocks are trading at. Figure 1 shows the holdings breakdown of EWL. While the high asset allocation to the health care, consumer defensive, and financial sector looks dangerous at first, one has to understand that the first two are very defensive sectors and that the high allocation to them is rather conservative than risky.

Figure 2 shows the top 10 holdings of the ETF. Here, the true risk is shown at an asset allocation of more than 10% to Nestle SA, Roche Holding AG, and Novartis AG. An asset allocation like this is everything but safe, as it does not sufficiently diversify individual risk. When looking at the companies included in Figure 2 more deeply, one also wonders about the ETFs' high valuation. Nestle, for example, shows negative revenue and almost no EBITDA growth. Roch Holdings has at least a revenue growth of 3.23% (industry median of 6.86%) and a standard EBITDA growth of 8.6%. While Novartis AG is the only company weighted above 10% that shows above-average revenue growth, the question arises if a portfolio of Swiss value stocks with low growth should have the almost same P/E and P/B ratio as the S&P 500 that is known as the best index and comprised of value and growth stocks. My clear answer to the question would be no.

Economics

Currently, Switzerland shows solid GDP growth of 1.5% higher than the average growth since 2022 but in the lower range of the 2015-2020 period. At an unemployment rate of 2.9%, the labor market looks healthy. However, when looking at Figure 3, which depicts the unemployment rate over time, one can see a clear uptrend, suggesting possible future problems in this sector. Nevertheless, at an inflation rate of 0.3%, Switzerland is very healthy and has every opportunity to cut interest rates to elevate growth and reduce unemployment should this become a problem. At an additionally low debt/GDP rate of only 37.9%, Switzerland is incredibly low for a developed country. Furthermore, the country runs a trade and budget surplus, highlighting further financial stability. With exports making up roughly 75% of GDP, Switzerland is quite trade-dependent. Nevertheless, the country is very diversified in this space, with the biggest export partners being the US (16.3%), Germany (13.4%), and China (11%), followed by many EU countries. This protects Switzerland from the negative impacts of tariffs in comparison to other countries, which are far more dependent on relationships with the US. In particular, the trading with EU partners should be seen as very stable.

Politics

The current government of Switzerland consists of 4 parties due to their unique consensus-based model of governance. Currently, they focus on investment in pharma, machinery, and high-tech to counterbalance lower demand from Germany as a trade partner. Additionally, they aim for the ratification of new EU agreements to secure further market access, which is important due to the heavy EU reliance on exports, as already discussed. With more than 30% of EWL being health care, this should be generally good for stocks included in the ETF. While the far-right Swiss People's Party (SVP/UDC) won the last election with 29% of votes, they are far less extremist than comparable parties in Germany or France. Nevertheless, they are in favor of anti-immigration controls and reject EU agreements. However, as they cannot rule the country by themselves, politics in Switzerland is unlikely to shift. Meanwhile, the far-left had less than 1% of votes during the last election and, therefore, is negligible.

Conclusion

Switzerland could be every investor's dream when searching for a stable investment environment outside of the US. With great economic data, Switzerland is very stable and can cut rates if necessary. Sadly, EWL is very expensive, which is why I cannot give the ETF anything other than a “hold” rating. Nevertheless, the Swiss stock market might be interesting in the future when it's cheaper. Additionally, single stock picks could be interesting for value investors.

data.title: Alibaba: What Living In China Taught Me Over The Past Decade And Why I Am Buying
data.description:
Last week, I found myself in Shanghai, a city that I've called home for seven of the past ten years. It was a nostalgic return. I met up with a few of my Chinese investing buddies at my favorite BBQ spot. Over many Tsingtao beers, we discussed for hours the real-estate bubble, the shifting role of real estate in Chinese people's retirement planning, and what comes next.

When it came to Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF), I realized how much has changed since Charlie Munger made headlines with his BABA stake. Back then, I also took a small position, but as a value investor, I was skeptical of the "Empire Building" strategy. I am not a fan of throwing money at everything in the hope of becoming the leader in everything. I now see BABA as a leaner, more focused company that cares about value creation for shareholders.

This article represents my deep dive into that transformation, my experience with Alibaba and its competitors, where I think the risks still lie, and why I made it my largest Asian position. My perspective comes from using Taobao, Tmall, JD, Meituan, WeChat, and PDD apps daily - though it was mostly my wife doing all the shopping. Alongside that, I've spent countless hours in discussions with my Chinese friends who know these ecosystems inside out.

Macro Context, Tariffs, Misconceptions, and a National Rebalancing

Despite living in China, I wasn't clever enough to foresee the scale and impact of the regulatory crackdown. Many Western investors still see Chinese regulations as unpredictable, and destructive, and as a reason to avoid Chinese stocks completely. In reality, it was only about aligning the market conditions closer to the standards already enforced in the West: anti-monopoly actions, data security, protection of user rights, etc. These weren't communist attacks; they were corrections.

Coming from the crackdown era, we are now in the middle of a small U.S.-China "cold war," for which China has been preparing since Trump's first election victory. Anti-China narratives in U.S. media are intensifying. While Alibaba's exposure to the U.S. is minimal, these conflicts might have a broader impact. Pinduoduo (PDD Holdings) (PDD) might, for example, change its focus to the Chinese market, intensifying the e-commerce competition.

But here is what the negative narratives miss: Chinese households are saving ~44% of their incomes, compared to the U.S. at ~4.5%. These huge accumulated savings might provide a future tailwind when consumers' confidence increases back to pre-COVID levels.

At the same time, financial behavior is transforming. Every time I mentioned stocks to normal Chinese people, they used the words "playing stocks." They always saw it as gambling, while real estate has been the way to save for retirement. As the property market slows, interest in capital markets grows, both domestically and internationally. The government is actively encouraging investment diversification and finally liberalizing channels for international investing.

For Alibaba, this creates two potential tailwinds:

  • Huge savings from domestic retail investors might hit the market, with number one investments in Chinese mega-tech, increasing Alibaba's access to capital.
  • The most simple way for Chinese retail to invest is via apps like AliPay, which increases fee revenues.

Transformation from Empire Builders to Value Creators

When I look back at Alibaba from a few years ago, using the cash flow from high-margin e-commerce platforms to do M&A of everything that can grow the top line seemed like the main goal. Net income margins suffered, falling all the way from 46% in 2015 to 25% in 2021, to a current ~16%.

In 2023-2024, the tone finally changed, with management starting the fat trimming. They sold off physical retail chains like Sun Art and InTime, which were both revenue contributors with chronically low to no margins.

Margins trajectory looked catastrophic for years, but finally began stabilizing. Core e-commerce (Taobao and Tmall) remains highly profitable, with EBITDA margins ~45%.

Competition with JD, PDD, and Douyin

Chinese e-commerce is not for the weak. It's like a brutal contact sport where competitors are willing to lose money for years to gain market share.

JD.com (JD)

JD runs a very different model. It buys and holds the inventory in its own warehouses. One evening, my mouse broke. I took my phone, opened the JD app, and ordered a new one. Since I was already in the app, I grabbed a new headset as well. Not even one hour later, a delivery guy was ringing my bell. Both items are in hand for a low price and flawless service, and when you try to tip the guy, he politely refuses. That's JD - brutally efficient and reliable. But this is a low-margin business with ~5% EBIT margins, way below Alibaba's asset-light Taobao and Tmall.

Alibaba leverages Cainiao to match the logistics speed and dominates in high-margin categories. JD wins on reliability, and Alibaba wins on merchant diversity and user stickiness.

Pinduoduo

Pinduoduo is the more recent but serious disruptor. The inventor of "social commerce" leverages group buying to capture budget-conscious users. In 2016, no one thought anyone could ever challenge Alibaba in China. By 2020, PDD had more users.

But PDD and BABA are very different. PDD users spend much less per order. PDD's ecosystem is much narrower, with a model based on massive subsidies and very tight cost controls. Alibaba, on the other side, has a full logistics, payments, cloud, and advertising ecosystem that monetizes users at much higher margins.

Douyin (ByteDance) (BDNCE)

Douyin adds another layer to the competition with its TikTok and impulsive buying strategy. I've never used it, but I observed people in my office falling into online shopping addiction. It's entertaining, but usually disappointing when you receive the goods. Simply, it's great for sales volume. Terrible for product satisfaction.

Douyin is number one in attention capture but lacks infrastructure, customer support, and reliability. Alibaba's live streams like "Taobao Live" don't just chase clicks, they are fully integrated with their ecosystem of merchants, services, and fulfillment.

To wrap it up, each rival attacks a different front but does not replicate what Alibaba does.

The Global Push - AIDC

With a high domestic market saturation, future user base growth must come from abroad. Alibaba knows that and is reaching the global markets through its AIDC divisions - AliExpress, Lazada, Trendyol, and Daraz.

Despite leaving China, I live across Asia and use Lazada as my number one option for most things. But, compared to e-commerce quality services in China, Lazada falls very short - deliveries are much slower, sometimes damaged, and customer service lags. In my opinion, Alibaba still has a long way to go in improving its services abroad. They know how, but the lower competition doesn't push them to spend on improvement.

AliExpress has become an Amazon of Europe with local warehousing.

Cainiao supports all of this - a world-class logistics network. Its IPO was put aside due to weak conditions, but I don't mind it at all, as I believe in the long-term value of such a network.

Alibaba Cloud and AI

The Cloud is the backbone of Alibaba's AI ambitions. In 2024, it returned to strong revenue growth and, more importantly, expanded margins due to AI workloads, with EBITDA soaring 115% YoY. Their LLM, Tongyi Qianwen, is now being used across the whole ecosystem.

Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) and Huawei compete, but Alibaba remains the top commercial cloud choice.

So, is it profitable yet? Not massively, but it’s scaling.

As I see it, the investment in cloud and AI is not optional for Alibaba. They must invest or risk being behind the competition in everything they do, as AI affects everything that happens in their ecosystem - from logistics optimization to content personalization. Unlike past bets, this is defensive capex with offensive upside.

Valuation and Shareholders Reward

As my core thesis says, Alibaba has evolved from a top-line grower into a company rewarding its shareholders. With over $25 billion in net cash, the company is rewarding shareholders with a $25 billion buyback program and a $1/share dividend.

As Ben Graham wrote: "In the short run, the stock market is a voting machine. But in the long run, it is a weighing machine."

Despite the already low forward PE ratio of ~12, it would be a mistake to value BABA on this multiple. We know Alibaba is investing ~$53 billion into the AI infrastructure. The future return from this investment will determine if Alibaba turns out to be a great investment.

But I would still argue that:

  • Taobao and Tmall, which generated ~$27 billion EBITA in 2024, are worth close to Alibaba's enterprise value of ~$225 billion. Give them a sector median multiple of 8.8x, and the value gets to $230 billion.
  • Rapidly growing Cloud with a $16 billion 2024 revenue, making $430 million EBITA per quarter with expanding margins, can easily be valued at $40 billion, while the revenue multiple is still half of Amazon's AWS.
  • Cainiao was bought back on a $10 billion valuation by Alibaba, but its value might be much higher.
  • AIDC, with 32% revenue growth, while not yet contributing to EBITA, made ~$17.6 billion in 2024 revenue. If it reaches even half of its Chinese e-commerce margins, it could make $3.5 billion EBITA and be worth at least $15 billion.
  • Finally, we have Ant Group, which may be worth another $20 billion.
  • Local services and other non-categorized items are still making over $40 billion in revenue, and their value is not recognized inside the group.

Overall, with the current BABA share price, you are paying for their Chinese e-commerce. Every single other segment, even if not yet profitable, is worth more than zero. Summing up the minimum valuation for each will give you at least 50% upside to fair value.

Let’s consider three 5-year outlooks and the valuation it supports:

  • Base case: Margins slightly expand, Cloud grows, Cainiao IPOs — FCF supports ~$150/share.

  • Bull case: Margins expand, Cloud hits 20% EBIT, Cainiao & AIDC spin-off — $180+ valuation.

  • Bear case: Regulatory drag, increased competition, poor execution — FCF stalls, but downside cushioned by cash and assets ($90–100).

In short, I would say that the downside is covered. You are buying a stable e-commerce cash-generating machine with several growth engines that come for free.

Final Words

Despite my bullish stance, it doesn't come without risks: regulatory overreach, U.S.-China tensions, VIE structure, delisting threats, and fierce competition all remain real risks. Execution on AI, Cloud, and AIDC could disappoint. But all of this is known, and I believe, more than priced in, cushioned by Alibaba's fortress balance sheet.

Alibaba today is leaner, more shareholder-friendly, more disciplined, and more focused. The empire-building phase is over.

It is a strong buy for me, but still with a disciplined sizing. Going overweight makes sense only with access to the Hong Kong exchange, knowledge of Chinese dynamics, and conviction to withstand the next inevitable doom cycle of Western media. If that conviction isn't there, I see nothing wrong by going long China in a more diversified way via the KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: St. Joe: Decades Of Value Creation Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Warren Buffett says that price is what you pay and value is what you get, and I think this phrase applies really well to St. Joe (NYSE:JOE). I don't usually analyze real estate businesses because I don't consider it my strong point so far, but after coming across this company and starting to research it, I believe we might be looking at a very interesting opportunity. The short summary of the thesis is that by buying St. Joe shares, it's like we're buying acres in Florida for 5-10 times less than what they're actually worth. We pay X for acres that are worth 5X-10X, which is why Warren Buffett's famous quote fits so well in this case.

At first glance, St. Joe isn't a company that draws much attention. The stock has been flat for many years and its business requires a lot of patience, something that's not very abundant on Wall Street. However, I think if we analyze the current state of the business, we can see that the years the stock has remained flat have probably meant that a huge gap has opened up between the share price and the real value of the business. In this article, I'll try to explain why.

Bay-Walton Area

Before we go into the specifics of the company's assets, I think it’s almost more important to get a handle on where these assets are actually located. For me, that’s the real key to this whole thing.

St. Joe owns most of its land and properties in northwest Florida, specifically in Bay and Walton counties. As we’ll see, this area is pulling in tons of new residents every year and is growing like crazy, which is huge for St. Joe’s interests. I mean, what’s the point of having thousands of acres if they’re stuck in some desert where nobody wants to live? To be specific, St. Joe’s got about 168,000 acres in this region, and 25% of them are near the coast or the sea, which we’ll talk about in a sec.

This part of the U.S., like pretty much all of Florida, is famous for its awesome weather year-round, especially in winter. While people up north are shivering in below-10-degree Fahrenheit weather for weeks, down here it barely drops below 45, even on the worst days. And since it’s so easy to move around the country these days, the climate’s becoming a bigger deal for folks picking a new place to live. This area’s got a massive edge because of that. There’s an image coming up that shows the average weather in Panama City, one of the big cities around here.

Another thing drawing people to Florida is the low taxes and cheaper cost of living. A bunch of folks are ditching pricey, high-tax states like California and New York for places like Texas or Florida, where living costs and taxes are way lower. Now, if there’s a downside to this spot, it’s that being in Florida means hurricanes are a thing. And honestly, the northwest part of the state is one of the most hurricane-prone areas.

Still, it seems like the good stuff outweighs that risk by a mile, and people are flocking here. Walton and Bay counties have seen insane population growth over the last few decades. In Bay, the population’s doubled since 1980, and in Walton, it’s quadrupled since then.

The average family income in these counties is around $90k in Walton and $80k in Bay (I’m guessing that $8k in the original was a typo!), while the median home price is $376,400 in Walton and $276,900 in Bay. Like we said earlier, housing here is pretty affordable compared to other parts of the U.S. The family income might not be the highest in the country, but it lines up nicely with the cost of living. You can live way better here than in places where salaries are higher, but the cost of living is totally out of control.

Oh, and another big plus? Thanks to its location and killer weather, this area’s a hotspot for tourism, especially for Americans. I couldn’t dig up solid tourist numbers, but we’ve got a decent clue from the Panama City-Bay County airport. That place went from 80,000 passengers a year in 2014 to over 170,000 in 2023—more than doubling in just 10 years. That’s gotta be a good sign that people are getting more and more interested in this region.

The Bay-Walton plan

So, another big thing to know about is the Bay-Walton plan, which got the green light back in 2015. It's basically an extension of the old West Bay Sector Plan. This is a massive, long-term development plan – we're talking a 50-year vision here – that's supposed to guide growth, development, and protect the environment in Bay and Walton counties.

The plan covers about 110,500 acres owned by The St. Joe Company. Most of that land, like 88% of it (that's 97,216 acres), is in Bay County, and the other 12% (13,284 acres) is in Walton County. And get this – over half of that, like 53,000 acres, is set aside for protecting important natural resources.

They're rolling out the plan bit by bit, depending on how the market's doing, through these Detailed Specific Area Plans (DSAPs). Each of these DSAPs has to cover at least 1,000 acres and give the lowdown on land use, infrastructure, nature, and transport.

The plan's got all sorts of development zones – like a Regional Employment Center, Business Center, West Bay Center, Town Center, Village Center, low-impact residential areas, and Long-Term Preservation areas. Plus, they're throwing in over 100 miles of public trails that'll link up with existing regional networks, so you can hike and bike all you want.

Getting this plan approved basically means St. Joe can keep developing land for at least the next 50 years, and it sets up a long-haul strategy for the whole region's growth. St. Joe themselves say they've got the legal go-ahead to build 170,000 homes, 3,000 hotel rooms, and over 22 million square feet of space for businesses and industries.

How do they earn money?

Before valuing St. Joe Company’s assets, it’s essential to understand the developments the company has in operation or in progress. These assets represent a significant portion of its current value. The company structures its operations and generates revenue through three main business segments: Hospitality, Residential, and Commercial.

Hospitality

This segment focuses on capitalizing on the tourist appeal of Northwest Florida, as previously discussed, through the development and management of a portfolio of leisure and accommodation-oriented properties. St. Joe creates and operates hotels, both luxury and more accessible, high-end golf clubs, and marinas. The goal is to attract a broad spectrum of visitors and generate revenue through hotel operations, club memberships, and related services.

Examples of properties include hotels like The Pearl Hotel, WaterColor Inn, and Camp Creek Inn, golf clubs such as Camp Creek and Shark’s Tooth, the exclusive Watersound Club, and marinas like Point South Marina in Port St. Joe. The company has significantly expanded this segment, recently opening several hotels, reaching a total of 12 hotels now in operation. I recommend checking Google Maps for some of the properties mentioned to get a better sense of the area’s appeal and the quality of the properties, though the following image should give you a good idea.

Residential

The residential segment is dedicated to developing various types of housing communities to meet the demand for homes for a growing population in the area. It also manages the strategic sale of developed land, both directly and through joint ventures, moving away from selling large tracts of undeveloped land.

St. Joe develops everything from luxury communities like Watersound Camp Creek to active adult communities (55+) like Latitude Margaritaville Watersound, as well as workforce housing in developments like College Station and Titus Park, and single-family homes in planned communities such as Breakfast Point East and Ward Creek.

Commercial

This segment focuses on creating and managing a diverse portfolio of commercial properties for lease, generating recurring rental income. St. Joe develops properties to meet various commercial, residential, and service needs within its planned communities and the region.

The portfolio includes shopping centers (such as Watersound Town Center and Pier Park North), industrial spaces and business parks, office buildings, retail spaces, apartments (like a 240-unit community in Panama City), senior living facilities, medical centers (such as the FSU/TMH medical campus), and self-storage units. The strategy aims to maintain high occupancy rates to ensure stable revenue streams.

Valuation

Now comes perhaps the most complicated part: valuing all of St. Joe's assets and trying to determine if the price we pay for the company today offers potential or not. First, we will value the developments that are already generating cash flows, and then the remaining land.

Analyzing the three segments reveals spectacular growth across all of them in recent years. There's slightly more fluctuation in the residential segment because land sales aren't evenly distributed each year, somewhat distorting the figures. Even so, the growth is clearly excellent in all three areas.

Taking into account the operating margins and general and administrative expenses, we arrive at a value for the company's operating businesses of about $1.8 billion. It should be noted that the multiples used are, in my opinion, very conservative. Some segments could easily be valued at more than 15x EBIT given the recurrence and predictability of these cash flows, but as I mentioned, I prefer to be extremely conservative to leave a good margin of safety.

Next, for valuing the undeveloped land, I've decided to divide the acreage into 4 groups, as clearly not all the company's land holds the same value. First, to the 53,000 acres protected by the Bay-Walton Sector Plan, I have assigned a value of $5k per acre. This value seems extremely conservative to me, as the average acre in Florida is worth around $33-36k, but given that there won't be major developments on this land, I prefer to assign them minimal value.

Then, I decided to divide the remaining acres into 3 groups: high, medium, and low quality. An acre on the coast can be worth over $250k, and there have even been transactions for over $1M. So, I have assumed that 10% of the remaining acres are of very high quality, meaning located by the sea, lakes, or important cities. Then 40%, I have valued as medium-quality acres, meaning near the prime quality ones but somewhat further from important areas or close to roads or industries. To these acres, I have assigned $40k each, slightly above the state average but well below the value of acres in similar areas. The rest I have valued at $15k per acre, less than half the state average, as they are in less desirable areas for residential or commercial development

Therefore, being extremely conservative with the price per acre, we arrive at a value that could currently exceed $4 billion. It is also important to consider that inflation and the fact that it is an in-demand area, as we have seen, will increase the value of these acres annually.

Adding the value of both parts and subtracting the debt, we arrive at a value for St. Joe of around $5.5 billion, although its current market capitalization is about $2.5 billion. Therefore, by valuing both its operating part and future developments very conservatively, we obtain a valuation double the current one. In fact, if we subtract the $1.7Bn from the operating business value, we can say that by buying St. Joe, we are acquiring 168,000 acres in one of Florida's most attractive areas for about $4,500 per acre, which is nearly 10 times less than the state average.

Perhaps, the only thing required for this value to be realized is patience. The developments will gradually occur, and the cash flows will arrive, but we must be more patient than the market. Currently, they generate about $107M in FFO, which means the company trades at about 22x FFO. Therefore, buying St. Joe means acquiring commercial, residential, and hospitality developments in a very high-demand area of Florida, at a quite reasonable price, and with what seems like an underappreciation of the company's future potential. For me, this company is a clear "Strong Buy" if you have enough patience for the developments to be completed.

data.title: Gladstone Commercial: A Great 9% Yielding Industrial REIT To Buy
data.description:
Gladstone Commercial Corporation (NASDAQ:GOOD) is a well-managed real estate investment trust that focuses primarily on the ownership and operation of an industrial-centric property portfolio.

Though Gladstone Commercial is predominantly focused on industrial net lease properties, which accounted for about 2/3rds of the REIT’s portfolio income as of December 31, 2024, the real estate investment trust owned a considerable amount of office properties as well.

Since investors recently soured on owning stocks in general, I think that the correction we have seen in the market as of late opens up an opportunity to buy Gladstone Commercial’s stock. Previously, in my piece titled ‘Gladstone Capital: 9% Yield, Improving Coverage, 1.12x NAV’, I highlighted positive trends in the eCommerce market specifically as being supportive of my bullish investment thesis.

I think that Gladstone Commercial will be able to pay its $0.30 per share per quarter dividend all throughout 2025, and I see a positive risk/reward relationship as the portfolio tilts further toward industrial properties.

Industrial-Centric Real Estate Portfolio

Gladstone Commercial has pursued a strategy of growing its industrial real estate portfolio in the last couple of years in a major attempt to shift its portfolio toward high-growth real estate assets. As the commercial real estate trust boosted its presence in the industrial real estate market, it simultaneously scaled back its office exposure, making Gladstone Commercial more and more of an industrial-centric real estate investment.

As of December 31, 2024, Gladstone Commercial’s industrial assets made up 63% of total income derived from its properties. Offices, now relegated to a distant number 2 in terms of income contribution, accounted for just 33% of portfolio income in the December-quarter.

Gladstone Commercial also owned smaller percentages of its portfolio invested in retail and medical offices, but these assets don’t make a substantial income contribution.

Gladstone Commercial has aggressively doubled down on its investments in industrial real estate in recent years, a move that was backed by the notion that a growing eCommerce market would require more investments in industrial real estate infrastructure, such as warehouses. In 2024, 63% of income in Gladstone Commercial’s real estate portfolio was produced by industrial real estate, up from 35% in 2018.

Gladstone Commercial also has a very well-occupied real estate portfolio that ensures the payment of a steady amount of funds from operations. In 2024, Gladstone Commercial’s occupancy was 98.7%, up 0.9 percentage points YoY.

Gladstone Commercial earned $0.35 per share in core funds from operations in the fourth quarter, reflecting a decrease of 2.8% YoY. The real estate investment trust’s dividend pay-out ratio in 4Q24 as 86% compared to 79% in 3Q24.

The dividend, as far as coverage is concerned, is quite well-covered, though Gladstone Commercial has the highest dividend pay-out ratio in the peer group of industrial REITs.

Gladstone Commercial is primarily focused on industrial REITs, and so are Broadstone Net Lease, Inc. (BNL), STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG) and W. P. Carey (WPC) whose LTM dividend pay-out ratios move between 61% to 84%, with the peer group average being 74%.

Gladstone Commercial had the highest LTM dividend pay-out ratio compared to its industrial REIT peers in 2024, but I do not think that investors need to be concerned about the dividend given the relative stability of its core funds from operations.

Industrial Real Estate Tailwinds

The eCommerce industry is anticipated to remain a booming industry in the coming years, which implies that warehouses, distribution centers and other industrial real estate properties should remain in high demand.

Growing eCommerce penetration rates, expected to grow from 22.5% in 2024 to 29.9% by 2028, hint at growing parcel volumes, which in turn should be a tailwind for industrial property utilization.

I think with eCommerce growth being a main driver of the industrial real estate industry, the prospects for rental growth and occupancy for REITs like Gladstone Commercial are leaning clearly on the positive side. Based on Moody’s information, industrial rents kept going up in 4Q24, reflecting 0.3% YoY growth.

Low FFO Multiple Reflects A High Margin Of Safety

Gladstone Commercial is a bargain based on 4Q24 run-rate core funds from operations. The real estate investment trust, based off of funds from operations, is the cheapest industrial REIT investment in the peer group as its stock is 14% cheaper than the average industrial real estate investment trust.

Gladstone Commercial is selling for 9.6x 4Q24 annualized core funds from operations, whereas Broadstone Net Lease is selling for a core FFO multiple of 9.9x. Both STAG Industrial and W.P. Carey have considerably higher Price-to-FFO ratios, ranging between 12-13x FFO.

STAG Industrial is a unique REIT in the peer group because it is 100% focused on industrial real estate and is a solid REIT investment if you desire steady, monthly dividend income. Since STAG Industrial is also focused fully on the industrial real estate market, it is the only pure play industrial REIT in the peer group here.

W.P. Carey is also focused on warehouses and industrial properties as a main source of producing rental income, but the trust retains a big position (22%) of retail investments (in the United States and abroad), so the real estate investment trust offers investors a much better real estate portfolio. The price for this better diversification, however, is a higher FFO multiple.

In my view, Gladstone Commercial should be able to re-rate to a 10-11x funds from operations multiple as the portfolio is performing well, producing predictable core FFO and has robust portfolio lease metrics, and I anticipate the real estate investment trust to prioritize growth in the fast-growing industrial real estate class moving forward. Thus, the trust’s implied intrinsic value is $15.40 at the high point, and $14.00 at the low point.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Turn Out To Be More Bearish

Gladstone Commercial is increasingly oriented toward industrial real estate, which makes the trust more of a concentrated bet on this particular real estate asset class.

If Gladstone Commercial is going to continue to overweight industrial real estate, the REIT is obviously going to be more vulnerable to a possible downturn in the eCommerce industry and could potentially miss out on higher returns in other markets, such as U.S. offices.

My Conclusion

Passive income vehicles such as Gladstone Commercial have become considerably more compelling as income investments after the recent market carnage.

In my view, the overweighting of industrial real estate has made strategic sense for Gladstone Commercial as the real estate investment trust’s industrial real estate assets profoundly profit from growing eCommerce transactions and a broadening real estate infrastructure to support the growth of the industry.

Though Gladstone Commercial has the highest core FFO-based dividend pay-out ratio in the peer group, I think that the real estate investment trust offers passive income investors a well-covered dividend and should be in a good position to maintain its $0.30 per share per quarter dividend pay-out.

Moreover, the fundamentals in the industrial real estate market are looking robust, underscoring that Gladstone Commercial’s strategic shift into industrial properties is paying off.

data.title: Pfizer: Risks Priced In, Buy The Exceptional Valuation And Yield
data.description:
Pfizer Inc (NYSE:PFE) has had a troublesome few years after the revenue boom generated by its COVID vaccine. The company was among the few beneficiaries of the need to rapidly vaccinate massive swaths of the global population, competing chiefly against Moderna (MRNA) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) in the Western world. The rapid ascent of the company's revenue and EPS resulted in several months of exceptional stock returns. The success was short-lived, though. Once COVID infection rates normalized and have now hit a 'steady-state' flu-like infection cycle, Pfizer's once ludicrous sales growth quickly became overcome with burdensome internal bloat and looming patent cliffs. Several billions in revenue related to currently protected drugs are at risk as the company will face margin compression from generic drug proliferation. The assets at risk include Prevnar (2026), Ibrance (2027), Xtandi (2027) and Eliquis (2028). Further, the company's upcoming product pipeline recently took a hit as it announced that it will be halting the development of its GLP-1 weight loss drug. Deterioration in the company's total revenue, net margin, and growth prospects have created the perfect storm of headwinds for the stock, leading to abysmal performance:

Despite the horrible recent returns, Pfizer remains a key player in the global pharmaceutical industry, touts a very attractive gross margin (suggesting continued pricing power), has undergone a series of cost-cutting measures, and maintains a robust R&D pipeline bolstered by recent acquisitions. Adding to this, the company recently increased its dividend, now eclipsing a mouth-watering 7% yield. In this article, I'll discuss the nature of the pharmaceutical industry, Pfizer's plan to rise from the post-COVID ashes, and why I believe there's too much pessimism baked into the stock.

Big Pharma: Big R&D, Big Markups, and Patent Cliffs

The pharmaceutical industry has become prolific in the US as the United States people have become the most medicated population in the world. America's obsession with prescription medication is illustrated best in the recent craze over GLP-1 medications, which were initially intended as type-2 diabetes medications but found an enormous market in 'treating' obesity. The paradox here is clear: Americans want to take a pill to 'cure' obesity so that they don't need to make the requisite lifestyle changes to achieve this. This recent study found that patients without type-2 diabetes exhibited higher discontinuation rates after 1 year and lower reinitiation rates than those with type-2 diabetes. This suggests that these patients used GLP-1 drugs specifically to lose weight, stopped once they achieved some arbitrary weight loss goal, and didn't return to use. However, without changing their lifestyle, it's likely that these folks will face a high recidivism rate for obesity. It is Americans' desire for a quick, easy fix to life's ills that has driven the pharma industry to become enormous. This type of problematic behavior has led to the rise of a counterculture, typified by the current FDA chair Robert F. Kennedy, an outspoken adversary to vaccines.

The pharmaceutical industry itself has had a part to play in this. America is among the few countries globally that allows widespread marketing efforts for prescription drugs. The logic makes sense at a base level: big pharma companies have massive research expenditures to discover new drugs, so they need mechanisms to monetize successful discoveries to reinvest in future research. The ability to freely advertise new patent-protected medications priced at an enormous markup is the 'mechanism' in question. Over time patent protections expire, allowing generic or biosimilar drugs to saturate the market and drive costs down for consumers. This is good for consumers but decimates the previous monopolists' margin on a given drug. This necessitates aggressive investment in R&D to maintain a robust portfolio of high-margin, patented drugs.

This system should lead to more novel drug discoveries that can treat more diseases and improve aggregate health outcomes over time. The reality is much more nuanced, as I previously noted the bleak side of the American obsession with pharmaceutical drugs. I don't intend to debate the ethics of the current state of pharmacy in the US, rather I am making the claim that it's a system which I don't see changing overnight. From an investor's perspective, this makes big pharma companies a very attractive long-term investment opportunity.

Given that context, the most important analysis for a pharmaceutical stock is not of its current portfolio but of its development pipeline.

Pfizer's R&D Pipeline, Bolstered by Acquisitions

Pfizer management is not blind to the bear case of the stock. Patent cliffs are an existential threat to any Big Pharma company and a reality that they've all had to grapple with. Those that remain dominant today, Pfizer included, are well-versed in these types of scenarios. That's why the company announced aggressive cost-cutting measures post-COVID and throughout 2027 aiming to eliminate $5b in costs. While corporate rightsizing has very real effects on employee morale, a return to profitability can quickly re-invigorate employees who have significant stock-based compensation. The company rewarded employees with $877m in stock-based comp in 2024 alone, ensuring the remaining employees have the incentive to keep the R&D engine pumping.

To its credit and despite analyst negativity, Pfizer maintains a pipeline of 100+ drugs in development, with a particular focus on Oncology and Biosimilar treatments. Much of the Oncology pipeline was acquired in the recent Seagen acquisition, after which the CEO Albert Bourla commented, “We believe Oncology will be a significant growth driver for Pfizer and contribute meaningfully to the achievement of our near- and long-term financial goals”.

The Seagen acquisition doubled Pfizer's Oncology pipeline, now at 59, with 8 blockbuster drugs now expected by 2030. The company also recently launched an RSV vaccine named Abrysvo and an ulcerative colitis treatment called Velsipity. The company expects these to be material contributors to sales by 2030. Further, Pfizer is working on a joint flu/COVID vaccine that could generate meaningful annual revenues during flu/COVID season and offers better unit economics than providing a vaccine for either of them individually.

Overall, Pfizer's product pipeline and strategic initiatives exist in stark contrast to beaten down market sentiment. The company is not some fledgling biotech dependent on one blockbuster drug to catapult its profits. It is a stalwart of the American pharmaceutical industry that is not going to zero. While recent sales growth has been abysmal, the company offers an exceptionally high yield and an extremely attractive valuation considering the product pipeline. Current valuation metrics suggest very limited business potential, while reality exists in stark contrast to this.

Financials

Pfizer currently trades at around a $125b market cap, about 2x 2024 sales of $63b. The company enjoys a prosperous 74% gross margin in 2024, up 4 percentage points from 2023. Despite this, 2024 results still present risks. Most notably is the $67b in total debt, resulting in a $3b interest expense in 2024. The company's $2.2b net interest expense was roughly 12.5% of 2024 operating income, so deleveraging is a key priority moving forward.

Besides that, I see the balance of patent cliffs and upcoming blockbuster releases as broadly offsetting each other from a margin perspective. The company will need to rely on sales growth to generate EPS growth and significant stock returns. However, investors today are more than reimbursed for taking on the risks inherent, as the stock costs about $22.50 and pays an annual dividend of $1.72. This translates to a yield in excess of 7.5% and a quarterly dividend that was recently increased from $0.42 to $0.43. The safest dividend is that one that was just increased. Management and the board are still clearly confident in Pfizer's long-term ability to generate profitable growth. I broadly agree with management, and therefore believe right now is an exceptional opportunity to initiate a position in Pfizer.

Investor Takeaway

Pfizer is a titan of the pharmaceutical industry that has faced several material headwinds in recent years. However, the current bearish narrative is more driven by stock returns than fundamental issues with the company. For this reason, I'm initiating my coverage of Pfizer stock with a Strong Buy on the very compelling 7x PE and exceptional dividend yield.

data.title: OPTZ: Not The Best Investment In A Tariff Sensitive Market
data.description:
As many investors in my place would be, I am usually very skeptical of ETFs that follow a passive investment strategy but nevertheless charge 'active rates' - and then struggle to deliver ongoing alpha. On the surface, Optimize Strategy Index ETF (NASDAQ:OPTZ) seems to be such a fund: the selection criteria are mostly quantitative and very specific, which makes it easier to manage; a higher-than-normal expense ratio for a passive fund, which I find concerning; and, finally, a performance profile that is unimpressive, at best.

OPTZ: Purpose and Strategy

The purpose of the fund is clearly spelled out:

...allows individual investors to participate in the long-term growth of innovative American companies. Over many decades, the U.S. stock market has rewarded those who have shown unyielding faith in the power of successful companies to grow shareholder value.

One might find it intriguing that an ETF would use profound phrases like "unyielding faith" to promote itself and advance its agenda, but that intrigue only lasts until one understands the filtering criteria for its cache of 306 equity holdings. These criteria essentially answer the question "how do you pick innovative, successful, American companies that will grow shareholder value over a long time-window?"

This is how the ETF makes its picks, as I understand it:

  1. The first step is to assess specific fundamentals - current earnings per share, ten-year average EPS, two years' forward growth for EPS, and price to sales data
  2. Having graded these metrics using what I can reasonably assume is a proprietary quantitative model, the equity universe used for the selection is then divided into two groups - one on either side of the $2 billion market capitalization mark. Note that P/S is used for the sub-$2-billion group in addition to the other metrics
  3. The next step is the sector-wise grouping, following which the top 20 percent in each sector under each market cap grouping is selected for the fund to invest in.

The fund rebalances periodically, and since it celebrates its first birthday on April 22, we can use the 39 percent annualized turnover as an accurate yardstick for the intensity and aggressiveness of these rebalances.

I will say that I find it a little on the high side, but corporate earnings were volatile over 2024 so it is reasonable to expect a quant model to have kept making adjustments throughout the four quarters.

This is also why I find the 0.50 percent expense ratio to be unusually high. Passive funds tend to have fewer transactions and lower management fees, as is the case with ETFs like SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) offering 0.09 percent or even Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) at a higher 0.20 percent. Why should I pay half a percentage of my total return in exchange for a passive portfolio that is algorithmically chosen? That is the issue I have with this fund.

Alright, so the basics of the fund do not add up for me, but how effective has its strategy been in the past year since inception? We briefly saw that it was unimpressive, but it deserves a closer look in terms of where it deviated from the performance of its benchmark, S&P 500 Index (SP500).

The OPTZ Disadvantage

On a total return basis, the fund does not seem to have performed that badly. It did not beat the market, no, but it does seem to have kept ahead of the index and its total returns for much of its existence. Not coincidentally, at least in my view of things, this outperformance received a proper trouncing just weeks after Trump took office. This points to an inference I cannot ignore - the market suddenly decided to take a ride on the rollercoaster, punishing growth and value equities alike, indiscriminate in its slashing of market value across the spectrum. The tariff catalyst, whether it materializes or not, has been a most fearsome one for market participants.

That makes this an inappropriate time to invest in equities because the fund's diversified holdings seem to do very little to protect investors on the downside.

There no longer appears to be any value assigned to value, to put it bluntly.

Strong fundamentals, however, tend to bring an equity back to its fair value much more quickly once the market gains more visibility into the future. When that might happen is anybody's guess.

On the positive side of it, we know that the ETF did outperform SP500 in a relatively bullish market leading up to the disruption, so it does generate alpha returns when market sentiment is overall positive.

This is your cue. What you must decide, as an investor, is whether you possess the patience to keep your funds in relatively liquid assets (they pay a fair yield, you know) so there is adequate dry powder to acquire valuable equities at fire-sale prices if and when a recession hits - or, if you are brave enough to buy those same equities now via an ETF like OPTZ, which has the potential to outperform the market and possibly spring back healthier than before once the tariff winds subside.

The second approach obviously entails a much higher level of risk because we have no idea if a major drawdown is still on the cards for the broader market. The eyes and ears of the world are focused on the painfully piecemeal and abrupt delivery of the president's 'Trump-start' announcements on tariffs, so any major news item that breaks is likely to influence the market one way or another. I believe they call it gambling.

This is my reason for assigning a hold to OPTZ. It outperforms more than marginally when market sentiment is overall bullish, but takes an ugly turn during market shocks... and the market is extremely shock-sensitive right now. What the ETF says on its website seems true: this is only for investors with "unyielding faith in the power of successful companies to grow shareholder value". For ye of little faith, this is a Hold.
